title: Praxis Audiology (5343) — 200 Source Questions + 100 Independent Practice Questions + 200 Guided Review
description: Contains 200 original source questions, 100 assistant-authored independent practice questions, and 200 source-derived guided-review activities. The assistant audit found 204 distinct independent content kernels. Two independently authored 100-question diagnostic batches linked to a 12-chapter learning library, targeted skill practice, and an optional 120-question timed simulation. The official 5343 test currently has 120 selected-response questions in 120 minutes; AlloFlow practice is not an official form or score.
contentReview: Assistant audit completed: 300 independent-practice items contain 204 distinct content kernels and 96 parallel variants under the normalized test. 200 additional activities are guided reasoning transformations, not independent exam questions. The 500-distinct-question target is not met.
disclaimer: Independent preparation material. Not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS or ASHA. The official Praxis Audiology (5343) test currently has 120 selected-response questions in 120 minutes; the 100-item diagnostic batches are not official-length forms. Practice results are not official or scaled Praxis scores, pass predictions, credentials, clinical evaluations, diagnoses, medical or vestibular decisions, device fittings, treatment, legal advice, or substitutes for qualified supervision, urgent medical care, and current federal, state, local, licensure, certification, educational, health-care, privacy, consent, emergency, and reporting requirements.

items.prompt: What is the primary function of the ossicular chain?
choices: They transform acoustic energy and improve impedance matching between air and cochlear fluid.
They generate neural action potentials.
They analyze speech meaning.
They equalize endolymph and perilymph composition.
items.rationale: The middle-ear system transfers energy from air to fluid through area and lever relationships while preserving a usable acoustic signal.
choiceRationales: Correct. The middle-ear system transfers energy from air to fluid through area and lever relationships while preserving a usable acoustic signal.

Not the best answer. "They generate neural action potentials." misses or conflicts with the controlling audiology principle. The middle-ear system transfers energy from air to fluid through area and lever relationships while preserving a usable acoustic signal.

items.prompt: A patient has a focal lesion above the cochlear nuclei yet retains some audibility for signals presented to either ear. Which neural organization best accounts for this finding?
choices: Above the cochlear nuclei, ascending auditory projections remain strictly ipsilateral through the cortex.
Ascending auditory projections become extensively bilateral above the cochlear nuclei.
The facial nerve regenerates bilateral sensory input whenever one central auditory pathway is damaged.
Vestibular projections replace the damaged cochlear signal before it reaches the auditory brainstem.
items.rationale: Above the cochlear nuclei, substantial crossing and bilateral ascending projections allow input from either ear to reach structures on both sides. This organization supports binaural processing and helps explain why one rostral central lesion seldom eliminates all audibility from one ear.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Central auditory projections do not remain confined to the stimulated side after the cochlear nuclei; multiple crossings distribute information to both sides of the ascending system.

Correct. Above the cochlear nuclei, substantial crossing and bilateral ascending projections allow input from either ear to reach structures on both sides. This organization supports binaural processing and helps explain why one rostral central lesion seldom eliminates all audibility from one ear.
Not the best answer. The facial nerve supplies the stapedius efferent limb and facial motor functions; it does not regenerate bilateral afferent auditory input after a central lesion.

Not the best answer. Vestibular and cochlear afferents share cranial nerve VIII proximally, but vestibular projections do not replace damaged auditory input or account for preserved cortical audibility.

items.prompt: A 2000 Hz tone becomes less detectable when nearby noise activates overlapping cochlear filters, even though attention to the task is stable. Which mechanism best explains the threshold shift?
choices: Energetic masking from physical overlap between masker and target excitation.
Informational masking from uncertainty about which auditory object is the target.
Binaural summation from combining matching signals delivered to both ears.
Auditory adaptation from continued stimulation after the competing noise ends.
items.rationale: Energetic masking occurs when masker energy overlaps the auditory-filter excitation used to detect the target, reducing the target’s effective signal-to-masker relationship. The scenario holds attention stable and locates the interference in overlapping cochlear excitation rather than uncertainty or postexposure adaptation.
choiceRationales: Correct. The nearby masker activates overlapping auditory filters and reduces physical detectability of the target, which is the defining mechanism of energetic masking in this scenario.

Not the best answer. Informational masking reflects uncertainty, similarity, or difficulty selecting an auditory object beyond energetic overlap; the scenario instead specifies cochlear-filter interference with stable attention.
Not the best answer. Binaural summation can improve detection when related information is combined across ears and does not describe a nearby noise making this target less detectable.
Not the best answer. Adaptation concerns response change with continued stimulation or aftereffects and does not account for the simultaneous overlapping masker described here.

items.prompt: An infant passed newborn hearing screening, but later develops caregiver concerns and a risk factor associated with delayed-onset change. What is the appropriate next step?

items.rationale: Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Multiplying 200 Hz by three gives 600 Hz, whereas 400 Hz and 800 Hz represent the second and fourth harmonics and 67 Hz is a submultiple.
choiceRationales: Correct. Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Multiplying 200 Hz by three gives 600 Hz, whereas 400 Hz and 800 Hz represent the second and fourth harmonics and 67 Hz is a submultiple.

Not the best answer. Dividing the fundamental by three does not produce the third harmonic of a harmonic complex. Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Multiplying 200 Hz by three gives 600 Hz, whereas 400 Hz and 800 Hz represent the second and fourth harmonics and 67 Hz is a submultiple.

Not the best answer. Four hundred hertz is the second harmonic because it is two times the 200 Hz fundamental. Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Multiplying 200 Hz by three gives 600 Hz, whereas 400 Hz and 800 Hz represent the second and fourth harmonics and 67 Hz is a submultiple.

Not the best answer. Eight hundred hertz is the fourth harmonic because it is four times the fundamental. Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. Multiplying 200 Hz by three gives 600 Hz, whereas 400 Hz and 800 Hz represent the second and fourth harmonics and 67 Hz is a submultiple.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A 1000 Hz threshold is reported as 20 dB HL. What does the HL reference indicate?
choices: The sound pressure equals 20 dB SPL for every calibrated earphone and test frequency.
The level is expressed relative to the standardized reference-equivalent threshold for that frequency and transducer.
The listener detects a pressure that is twenty times the atmospheric reference pressure.
The level is twenty decibels above the listener’s uncomfortable loudness level.
items.rationale: The dB HL scale incorporates reference-equivalent threshold sound-pressure levels so that zero approximates normal threshold across frequencies for a specified transducer. It cannot be converted to a single identical SPL value without calibration data.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. dB HL is frequency- and transducer-referenced, so its numerical value is not universally identical to dB SPL. The dB HL scale incorporates reference-equivalent threshold sound-pressure levels so that zero approximates normal threshold across frequencies for a specified transducer. It cannot be converted to a single identical SPL value without calibration data.

Correct. The dB HL scale incorporates reference-equivalent threshold sound-pressure levels so that zero approximates normal threshold across frequencies for a specified transducer. It cannot be converted to a single identical SPL value without calibration data.

Not the best answer. A decibel value is logarithmic and dB HL is referenced to normative threshold, not directly to atmospheric pressure. The dB HL scale incorporates reference-equivalent threshold sound-pressure levels so that zero approximates normal threshold across frequencies for a specified transducer. It cannot be converted to a single identical SPL value without calibration data.

Not the best answer. HL does not use an individual uncomfortable level as its reference point. The dB HL scale incorporates reference-equivalent threshold sound-pressure levels so that zero approximates normal threshold across frequencies for a specified transducer. It cannot be converted to a single identical SPL value without calibration data.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Two equal-amplitude pure tones of the same frequency arrive 180 degrees out of phase at one location. What idealized result is expected there?
choices: Constructive interference doubles the instantaneous pressure throughout every cycle.
The frequencies combine to create a new tone exactly one octave above the originals.
Destructive interference produces substantial cancellation of the combined pressure waveform.
The phase difference changes both tones into broadband random noise.
items.rationale: Equal sinusoids separated by half a cycle have opposing instantaneous pressures, so ideal superposition cancels them at that point. Real rooms and unequal amplitudes may limit cancellation, but the governing acoustic principle is destructive interference.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Constructive summation occurs for in-phase signals, not signals separated by 180 degrees. Equal sinusoids separated by half a cycle have opposing instantaneous pressures, so ideal superposition cancels them at that point. Real rooms and unequal amplitudes may limit cancellation, but the governing acoustic principle is destructive interference.

Not the best answer. Superposition does not transform two same-frequency tones into an octave solely because phase differs. Equal sinusoids separated by half a cycle have opposing instantaneous pressures, so ideal superposition cancels them at that point. Real rooms and unequal amplitudes may limit cancellation, but the governing acoustic principle is destructive interference.

Correct. Equal sinusoids separated by half a cycle have opposing instantaneous pressures, so ideal superposition cancels them at that point. Real rooms and unequal amplitudes may limit cancellation, but the governing acoustic principle is destructive interference.

Not the best answer. A stable phase offset between sinusoids does not create a broadband random signal. Equal sinusoids separated by half a cycle have opposing instantaneous pressures, so ideal superposition cancels them at that point. Real rooms and unequal amplitudes may limit cancellation, but the governing acoustic principle is destructive interference.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A measurement microphone underreports output above 6000 Hz but is accurate at lower frequencies. Which instrument property is deficient?
choices: Its temporal integration is excessive because the room contains no reverberation.
Its interaural timing sensitivity is reduced because the microphone has one input port.
Its bone-conduction oscillator force level is too high at all test frequencies.
Its frequency response is not sufficiently flat across the required high-frequency measurement range.
items.rationale: A measurement transducer should represent the intended bandwidth predictably. Systematic underresponse only at high frequencies indicates a nonflat frequency response or bandwidth limitation, which must be corrected or accounted for before interpreting output.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Temporal integration and room reverberation do not specifically explain a repeatable high-frequency sensitivity roll-off. A measurement transducer should represent the intended bandwidth predictably. Systematic underresponse only at high frequencies indicates a nonflat frequency response or bandwidth limitation, which must be corrected or accounted for before interpreting output.

Not the best answer. Interaural timing is a binaural auditory cue, not a required single-microphone response property. A measurement transducer should represent the intended bandwidth predictably. Systematic underresponse only at high frequencies indicates a nonflat frequency response or bandwidth limitation, which must be corrected or accounted for before interpreting output.

Not the best answer. A measurement microphone and a bone oscillator are different transducers, and the error is frequency specific rather than global. A measurement transducer should represent the intended bandwidth predictably. Systematic underresponse only at high frequencies indicates a nonflat frequency response or bandwidth limitation, which must be corrected or accounted for before interpreting output.

Correct. A measurement transducer should represent the intended bandwidth predictably. Systematic underresponse only at high frequencies indicates a nonflat frequency response or bandwidth limitation, which must be corrected or accounted for before interpreting output.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Why can excessive room reverberation reduce speech intelligibility even when the overall measured sound level is adequate?
choices: Delayed reflections smear temporal and spectral speech cues and reduce the effective direct-sound contrast.
Reflections remove all low-frequency energy before it reaches a listener.
Reflections convert airborne speech into bone-conducted vibration at the listener.
Reflections increase visual access to the talker while leaving auditory cues unchanged.
items.rationale: Late-arriving copies of earlier speech segments overlap newer segments, degrading envelope transitions and consonant contrasts. Adequate level alone therefore does not guarantee intelligibility when the direct-to-reverberant relationship is poor.
choiceRationales: Correct. Late-arriving copies of earlier speech segments overlap newer segments, degrading envelope transitions and consonant contrasts. Adequate level alone therefore does not guarantee intelligibility when the direct-to-reverberant relationship is poor.

Not the best answer. Reverberation does not categorically eliminate the low-frequency portion of speech. Late-arriving copies of earlier speech segments overlap newer segments, degrading envelope transitions and consonant contrasts. Adequate level alone therefore does not guarantee intelligibility when the direct-to-reverberant relationship is poor.

Not the best answer. Room reflections remain primarily airborne acoustic energy rather than becoming bone-conduction input. Late-arriving copies of earlier speech segments overlap newer segments, degrading envelope transitions and consonant contrasts. Adequate level alone therefore does not guarantee intelligibility when the direct-to-reverberant relationship is poor.

Not the best answer. Reverberation affects the auditory signal and does not improve the listener’s visual view. Late-arriving copies of earlier speech segments overlap newer segments, degrading envelope transitions and consonant contrasts. Adequate level alone therefore does not guarantee intelligibility when the direct-to-reverberant relationship is poor.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A coupler measurement is repeated after an earphone is reseated, and the response changes markedly. What is the best interpretation?
choices: The listener’s auditory threshold changed even though no listener participated in the coupler test.
The acoustic coupling or seal is affecting the measured response and must be standardized before comparison.
The cochlear traveling wave adapted to the repeated stimulus presentation.
The decibel scale became linear because the earphone was removed once.
items.rationale: Coupler results depend on reproducible placement, seal, cavity, and transducer connection. A large change after reseating signals a coupling variable or equipment issue that should be resolved before attributing differences to device performance.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A coupler is an electroacoustic test fixture, so a behavioral threshold cannot explain the measurement change. Coupler results depend on reproducible placement, seal, cavity, and transducer connection. A large change after reseating signals a coupling variable or equipment issue that should be resolved before attributing differences to device performance.

Correct. Coupler results depend on reproducible placement, seal, cavity, and transducer connection. A large change after reseating signals a coupling variable or equipment issue that should be resolved before attributing differences to device performance.

Not the best answer. No biological cochlea is present in an electroacoustic coupler measurement. Coupler results depend on reproducible placement, seal, cavity, and transducer connection. A large change after reseating signals a coupling variable or equipment issue that should be resolved before attributing differences to device performance.

Not the best answer. Reseating a transducer cannot change the mathematical definition of a decibel. Coupler results depend on reproducible placement, seal, cavity, and transducer connection. A large change after reseating signals a coupling variable or equipment issue that should be resolved before attributing differences to device performance.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A listener can just distinguish 1010 Hz from a 1000 Hz comparison tone under fixed conditions. What does the 10 Hz difference estimate?
choices: An absolute hearing threshold expressed as the softest detectable pressure level.
A critical ratio calculated from the bandwidth of a masking-noise spectrum.
A frequency difference limen, or just-noticeable difference, for that listener and condition.
A speech-recognition score derived from the percentage of words repeated correctly.
items.rationale: A difference limen is the smallest change in a stimulus dimension that is detected according to a defined criterion. Here the relevant dimension is frequency, and the estimate applies to the particular baseline, level, procedure, and listener.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The task compares two audible frequencies rather than estimating the minimum audible level. A difference limen is the smallest change in a stimulus dimension that is detected according to a defined criterion. Here the relevant dimension is frequency, and the estimate applies to the particular baseline, level, procedure, and listener.

Not the best answer. No masking-noise level or bandwidth is provided for a critical-ratio estimate. A difference limen is the smallest change in a stimulus dimension that is detected according to a defined criterion. Here the relevant dimension is frequency, and the estimate applies to the particular baseline, level, procedure, and listener.

Correct. A difference limen is the smallest change in a stimulus dimension that is detected according to a defined criterion. Here the relevant dimension is frequency, and the estimate applies to the particular baseline, level, procedure, and listener.

Not the best answer. The discrimination task uses pure-tone frequency differences rather than speech materials. A difference limen is the smallest change in a stimulus dimension that is detected according to a defined criterion. Here the relevant dimension is frequency, and the estimate applies to the particular baseline, level, procedure, and listener.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Equal-loudness contours show that a low-frequency tone and a midfrequency tone may require different sound-pressure levels to sound equally loud. What does this demonstrate?
choices: Perceived loudness is determined by frequency and is unaffected by presentation level.
All audible frequencies have identical thresholds when expressed in dB SPL.
A listener can compare loudness only when the two tones share the same frequency.
Auditory sensitivity and perceived loudness vary with frequency as well as physical level.
items.rationale: The ear is most sensitive across a midfrequency region and generally needs more SPL at very low or very high frequencies for equivalent loudness. Equal-loudness data therefore separate physical pressure from perceptual magnitude.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Loudness changes with level; equal-loudness contours show an interaction rather than frequency alone. The ear is most sensitive across a midfrequency region and generally needs more SPL at very low or very high frequencies for equivalent loudness. Equal-loudness data therefore separate physical pressure from perceptual magnitude.

Not the best answer. Threshold in SPL varies substantially across frequency, particularly at the spectrum’s extremes. The ear is most sensitive across a midfrequency region and generally needs more SPL at very low or very high frequencies for equivalent loudness. Equal-loudness data therefore separate physical pressure from perceptual magnitude.

Not the best answer. Cross-frequency loudness matching is precisely the task represented by equal-loudness contours. The ear is most sensitive across a midfrequency region and generally needs more SPL at very low or very high frequencies for equivalent loudness. Equal-loudness data therefore separate physical pressure from perceptual magnitude.

Correct. The ear is most sensitive across a midfrequency region and generally needs more SPL at very low or very high frequencies for equivalent loudness. Equal-loudness data therefore separate physical pressure from perceptual magnitude.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A narrowband masker centered below a test tone interferes with the higher-frequency tone more than a comparable masker centered above it. Which pattern is illustrated?
choices: The upward spread of masking associated with asymmetric cochlear excitation at higher masker levels.
The precedence effect caused by a delayed copy arriving from a reflecting surface.
Binaural release from masking produced by different signals at the two ears.
Loudness adaptation measured after a prolonged tone in the absence of a masker.
items.rationale: Cochlear excitation from a lower-frequency masker can extend toward higher-frequency places, especially as masker level rises. This creates more interference above the masker frequency than an equal spectral separation below it.
choiceRationales: Correct. Cochlear excitation from a lower-frequency masker can extend toward higher-frequency places, especially as masker level rises. This creates more interference above the masker frequency than an equal spectral separation below it.

Not the best answer. The precedence effect concerns localization of direct and delayed sounds, not asymmetric spectral masking. Cochlear excitation from a lower-frequency masker can extend toward higher-frequency places, especially as masker level rises. This creates more interference above the masker frequency than an equal spectral separation below it.

Not the best answer. The scenario describes spectral placement, not interaural differences that yield binaural release. Cochlear excitation from a lower-frequency masker can extend toward higher-frequency places, especially as masker level rises. This creates more interference above the masker frequency than an equal spectral separation below it.

Not the best answer. A simultaneous masker is present, so the pattern is not isolated loudness adaptation. Cochlear excitation from a lower-frequency masker can extend toward higher-frequency places, especially as masker level rises. This creates more interference above the masker frequency than an equal spectral separation below it.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A listener localizes the first of two nearly identical sounds from different loudspeakers when the second follows after a brief delay. Which phenomenon supports this perception?
choices: The occlusion effect, in which bone-conducted low frequencies increase with a blocked ear canal.
The precedence effect, in which the leading wavefront dominates perceived location under short delays.
Loudness recruitment, in which perceived loudness grows rapidly above a cochlear threshold.
The Stenger effect, in which a louder same-frequency signal controls perception in a nonorganic test.
items.rationale: For sufficiently short delays, the auditory system fuses direct and reflected copies while weighting location toward the first-arriving sound. This precedence effect helps stabilize localization in ordinary reverberant environments.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The occlusion effect concerns bone-conducted sound and canal closure, not competing arrival times. For sufficiently short delays, the auditory system fuses direct and reflected copies while weighting location toward the first-arriving sound. This precedence effect helps stabilize localization in ordinary reverberant environments.

Correct. For sufficiently short delays, the auditory system fuses direct and reflected copies while weighting location toward the first-arriving sound. This precedence effect helps stabilize localization in ordinary reverberant environments.

Not the best answer. Recruitment concerns loudness growth and does not explain localization of direct and reflected wavefronts. For sufficiently short delays, the auditory system fuses direct and reflected copies while weighting location toward the first-arriving sound. This precedence effect helps stabilize localization in ordinary reverberant environments.

Not the best answer. The Stenger principle uses dichotic level differences and is not the room-localization effect described. For sufficiently short delays, the auditory system fuses direct and reflected copies while weighting location toward the first-arriving sound. This precedence effect helps stabilize localization in ordinary reverberant environments.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Speech understanding improves when target and masker cues differ between the ears compared with identical monaural information. Which mechanism is most relevant?
choices: Monaural spectral smoothing removes consonant information from both ears equally.
Auditory fatigue raises both-ear thresholds after every brief speech presentation.
Binaural unmasking uses interaural timing and level relationships to improve separation of target and masker.
Middle-ear muscle contraction selectively amplifies the target talker in one ear.
items.rationale: The central auditory system compares interaural timing, phase, and level information. When a target and masker produce different binaural patterns, these comparisons can yield a release from masking beyond either ear’s monaural information.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Removing spectral detail would generally impair, not explain, the benefit from interaural differences. The central auditory system compares interaural timing, phase, and level information. When a target and masker produce different binaural patterns, these comparisons can yield a release from masking beyond either ear’s monaural information.

Not the best answer. Fatigue is not the mechanism behind an immediate performance benefit from binaural cue differences. The central auditory system compares interaural timing, phase, and level information. When a target and masker produce different binaural patterns, these comparisons can yield a release from masking beyond either ear’s monaural information.

Correct. The central auditory system compares interaural timing, phase, and level information. When a target and masker produce different binaural patterns, these comparisons can yield a release from masking beyond either ear’s monaural information.

Not the best answer. The acoustic reflex does not selectively enhance a desired talker based on binaural spatial cues. The central auditory system compares interaural timing, phase, and level information. When a target and masker produce different binaural patterns, these comparisons can yield a release from masking beyond either ear’s monaural information.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A listener identifies a partly obscured familiar sentence more accurately than an equally audible string of unrelated syllables. Which factor best explains the advantage?
choices: The sentence necessarily has a higher calibrated sound-pressure level than the syllables.
The cochlear amplifier becomes stronger whenever words form a grammatical sentence.
The middle ear transmits familiar vocabulary while attenuating unfamiliar syllables.
Top-down linguistic context supplies redundant constraints that support auditory closure.
items.rationale: Familiar syntax, semantics, and lexical expectations constrain possible interpretations and help fill missing acoustic information. The benefit reflects top-down context and redundancy, not a guaranteed peripheral level difference.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The scenario states equal audibility and does not establish a physical-level advantage. Familiar syntax, semantics, and lexical expectations constrain possible interpretations and help fill missing acoustic information. The benefit reflects top-down context and redundancy, not a guaranteed peripheral level difference.

Not the best answer. Outer-hair-cell gain is not selectively increased by a sentence’s grammatical structure. Familiar syntax, semantics, and lexical expectations constrain possible interpretations and help fill missing acoustic information. The benefit reflects top-down context and redundancy, not a guaranteed peripheral level difference.

Not the best answer. Middle-ear transmission does not classify acoustic input according to lexical familiarity. Familiar syntax, semantics, and lexical expectations constrain possible interpretations and help fill missing acoustic information. The benefit reflects top-down context and redundancy, not a guaranteed peripheral level difference.

Correct. Familiar syntax, semantics, and lexical expectations constrain possible interpretations and help fill missing acoustic information. The benefit reflects top-down context and redundancy, not a guaranteed peripheral level difference.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Which task most directly evaluates spectral resolution rather than audibility alone?
choices: Detecting a tone in notched noise while the spectral gap around the tone is systematically varied.
Reporting whether a 1000 Hz tone is heard in quiet at progressively lower levels.
Repeating a list of familiar words presented well above threshold in a quiet booth.
Judging whether a visual light flashes before or after an audible click.
items.rationale: Notched-noise paradigms estimate the frequency selectivity of the auditory filter around a signal by changing the masker-free spectral region. Quiet detection and speech tasks do not isolate filter bandwidth in the same way.
choiceRationales: Correct. Notched-noise paradigms estimate the frequency selectivity of the auditory filter around a signal by changing the masker-free spectral region. Quiet detection and speech tasks do not isolate filter bandwidth in the same way.

Not the best answer. A quiet-tone threshold principally estimates audibility rather than auditory-filter width. Notched-noise paradigms estimate the frequency selectivity of the auditory filter around a signal by changing the masker-free spectral region. Quiet detection and speech tasks do not isolate filter bandwidth in the same way.

Not the best answer. Word recognition in quiet combines multiple abilities and does not isolate spectral resolution. Notched-noise paradigms estimate the frequency selectivity of the auditory filter around a signal by changing the masker-free spectral region. Quiet detection and speech tasks do not isolate filter bandwidth in the same way.

Not the best answer. An audiovisual timing judgment does not directly measure cochlear spectral selectivity. Notched-noise paradigms estimate the frequency selectivity of the auditory filter around a signal by changing the masker-free spectral region. Quiet detection and speech tasks do not isolate filter bandwidth in the same way.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A child hears two simultaneous talkers as separate streams when their fundamental frequencies and locations differ. Which perceptual process is involved?
choices: Tympanometric peak-pressure estimation separates the voices at the middle ear.
Auditory scene analysis groups and segregates sound using coherent acoustic cues.
Acoustic-reflex decay labels each talker according to semantic content.
Pure-tone averaging combines both voices into one behavioral threshold.
items.rationale: Auditory scene analysis uses cues such as harmonicity, onset, location, and temporal coherence to group components from a common source and segregate competing sources. This supports following one voice in a complex environment.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Tympanometry measures middle-ear admittance and does not perceptually organize talkers. Auditory scene analysis uses cues such as harmonicity, onset, location, and temporal coherence to group components from a common source and segregate competing sources. This supports following one voice in a complex environment.

Correct. Auditory scene analysis uses cues such as harmonicity, onset, location, and temporal coherence to group components from a common source and segregate competing sources. This supports following one voice in a complex environment.

Not the best answer. Reflex decay is a physiologic measure and does not perform semantic grouping. Auditory scene analysis uses cues such as harmonicity, onset, location, and temporal coherence to group components from a common source and segregate competing sources. This supports following one voice in a complex environment.

Not the best answer. A pure-tone average summarizes thresholds and cannot explain perceptual stream segregation. Auditory scene analysis uses cues such as harmonicity, onset, location, and temporal coherence to group components from a common source and segregate competing sources. This supports following one voice in a complex environment.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: An older adult’s thresholds remain stable, but performance declines when speech is rapidly time-compressed. Which interpretation is most appropriate?
choices: Stable thresholds prove that suprathreshold speech processing cannot have changed.
Time compression increases the listener’s cochlear frequency range and should improve recognition.
Reduced temporal and cognitive processing efficiency may affect performance beyond peripheral audibility.
The result by itself establishes a new conductive disorder in both middle ears.
items.rationale: Rapid speech places demands on temporal resolution, working memory, attention, and linguistic processing. Stable pure-tone sensitivity therefore does not rule out age-related or individual difficulty with degraded, accelerated speech.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Pure-tone thresholds do not exhaustively measure temporal, linguistic, or cognitive demands. Rapid speech places demands on temporal resolution, working memory, attention, and linguistic processing. Stable pure-tone sensitivity therefore does not rule out age-related or individual difficulty with degraded, accelerated speech.

Not the best answer. Compression reduces temporal cues and does not expand the cochlear frequency range. Rapid speech places demands on temporal resolution, working memory, attention, and linguistic processing. Stable pure-tone sensitivity therefore does not rule out age-related or individual difficulty with degraded, accelerated speech.

Correct. Rapid speech places demands on temporal resolution, working memory, attention, and linguistic processing. Stable pure-tone sensitivity therefore does not rule out age-related or individual difficulty with degraded, accelerated speech.

Not the best answer. A difficult time-compressed speech score alone does not diagnose conductive pathology. Rapid speech places demands on temporal resolution, working memory, attention, and linguistic processing. Stable pure-tone sensitivity therefore does not rule out age-related or individual difficulty with degraded, accelerated speech.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: Why should an infant’s auditory development be monitored with functional communication observations as well as device and threshold measures?
choices: Use functional observations mainly when calibrated responses are inconsistent, because stable access measures otherwise represent daily listening.
Treat aided thresholds and verified device targets as sufficient predictors of how communication will develop in ordinary routines.
Collect caregiver observations after clinic and device measures are complete, but keep them separate from longitudinal audiologic decisions.
Access measures do not by themselves show how the infant uses sound in interaction, language learning, and daily routines.
items.rationale: Thresholds and device verification estimate auditory access, while caregiver reports and observations reveal listening, communication, and participation in meaningful contexts. Integrating both supports developmentally responsive follow-up without treating either as sufficient alone.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Observations add contextual evidence even when calibrated access measures are stable; they are not merely a fallback for inconsistent responses. Thresholds and device verification estimate auditory access, while caregiver reports and observations reveal listening, communication, and participation in meaningful contexts. Integrating both supports developmentally responsive follow-up without treating either as sufficient alone.

Not the best answer. Access is necessary but does not capture the interacting language, learning, family, and environmental factors that shape functional development. Thresholds and device verification estimate auditory access, while caregiver reports and observations reveal listening, communication, and participation in meaningful contexts. Integrating both supports developmentally responsive follow-up without treating either as sufficient alone.

Not the best answer. Caregiver observations should be integrated with clinic measures because they reveal listening and participation across settings and over time. Thresholds and device verification estimate auditory access, while caregiver reports and observations reveal listening, communication, and participation in meaningful contexts. Integrating both supports developmentally responsive follow-up without treating either as sufficient alone.

Correct. Thresholds and device verification estimate auditory access, while caregiver reports and observations reveal listening, communication, and participation in meaningful contexts. Integrating both supports developmentally responsive follow-up without treating either as sufficient alone.
referenceNames: Scope of Practice in Audiology — ASHA

items.prompt: A worker moves among several noisy areas during a shift. Which instrument is best suited to estimating the worker’s accumulated personal noise dose?
choices: A properly configured personal noise dosimeter worn throughout the representative work period.
A tympanometer used before and after the shift to measure middle-ear pressure.
A clinical audiometer set to present a single 1000 Hz tone in a sound booth.
A stationary sound-level reading taken once in the quietest work area.
items.rationale: A personal dosimeter integrates changing sound levels over time near the worker’s hearing zone. Correct settings and a representative sampling period allow cumulative exposure to be evaluated more appropriately than a single area measurement.
choiceRationales: Correct. A personal dosimeter integrates changing sound levels over time near the worker’s hearing zone. Correct settings and a representative sampling period allow cumulative exposure to be evaluated more appropriately than a single area measurement.

Not the best answer. Tympanometry evaluates middle-ear admittance and cannot measure environmental exposure dose. A personal dosimeter integrates changing sound levels over time near the worker’s hearing zone. Correct settings and a representative sampling period allow cumulative exposure to be evaluated more appropriately than a single area measurement.

Not the best answer. An audiometer measures auditory sensitivity and is not an exposure-monitoring instrument. A personal dosimeter integrates changing sound levels over time near the worker’s hearing zone. Correct settings and a representative sampling period allow cumulative exposure to be evaluated more appropriately than a single area measurement.

Not the best answer. One quiet-area snapshot does not represent changing personal exposure across the full shift. A personal dosimeter integrates changing sound levels over time near the worker’s hearing zone. Correct settings and a representative sampling period allow cumulative exposure to be evaluated more appropriately than a single area measurement.

items.prompt: Production equipment is modified in a way that may increase employee noise exposure. What should the hearing-conservation team do next?
choices: Wait for several workers to report tinnitus before collecting any new exposure data.
Repeat representative exposure monitoring and update controls and program enrollment from the measured results.
Assume the previous measurements remain valid because the building location did not change.
Measure the loudest workstation once and apply that area reading to every employee affected by the equipment change.
items.rationale: Material equipment or process changes can invalidate prior exposure estimates. Representative remonitoring identifies affected employees and informs engineering, administrative, protection, training, and follow-up decisions under the applicable program.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Symptom reports are not a substitute for timely monitoring after a potentially important process change. Material equipment or process changes can invalidate prior exposure estimates. Representative remonitoring identifies affected employees and informs engineering, administrative, protection, training, and follow-up decisions under the applicable program.

Correct. Material equipment or process changes can invalidate prior exposure estimates. Representative remonitoring identifies affected employees and informs engineering, administrative, protection, training, and follow-up decisions under the applicable program.

Not the best answer. Equipment and process changes can alter exposure even when work remains in the same building. Material equipment or process changes can invalidate prior exposure estimates. Representative remonitoring identifies affected employees and informs engineering, administrative, protection, training, and follow-up decisions under the applicable program.

Not the best answer. One area snapshot cannot represent each worker’s level, duration, movement, and task pattern after a process change. Material equipment or process changes can invalidate prior exposure estimates. Representative remonitoring identifies affected employees and informs engineering, administrative, protection, training, and follow-up decisions under the applicable program.

Occupational Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation — OSHA

items.prompt: Two earplugs carry similar laboratory attenuation labels, but workers achieve very different protection in practice. Which action most directly addresses this discrepancy?
choices: Select the plug with the higher labeled attenuation and assume each worker receives that value without checking insertion or seal.
Subtract the package rating directly from each worker’s exposure estimate without applying the required rating method or checking fit.
Provide individualized fit training and, when available, field attenuation or fit-testing feedback.
Ask whether the plugs make the workplace sound quieter and treat that report as confirmation of adequate personal attenuation.
items.rationale: Real-world protection depends heavily on device selection, insertion, fit, consistency, and worker use. Individual coaching and fit-testing feedback can reveal inadequate seals and improve achieved attenuation beyond label-based selection alone.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A laboratory label does not establish the attenuation an individual worker achieves with a particular fit. Real-world protection depends heavily on device selection, insertion, fit, consistency, and worker use. Individual coaching and fit-testing feedback can reveal inadequate seals and improve achieved attenuation beyond label-based selection alone.

Not the best answer. Label-based calculations require the applicable method and still do not measure the protection achieved by an individual wearer. Real-world protection depends heavily on device selection, insertion, fit, consistency, and worker use. Individual coaching and fit-testing feedback can reveal inadequate seals and improve achieved attenuation beyond label-based selection alone.

Correct. Real-world protection depends heavily on device selection, insertion, fit, consistency, and worker use. Individual coaching and fit-testing feedback can reveal inadequate seals and improve achieved attenuation beyond label-based selection alone.

Not the best answer. Perceived quietness and comfort can inform counseling but do not quantify the fit or attenuation achieved. Real-world protection depends heavily on device selection, insertion, fit, consistency, and worker use. Individual coaching and fit-testing feedback can reveal inadequate seals and improve achieved attenuation beyond label-based selection alone.

items.prompt: Serial monitoring shows an early high-frequency change during treatment with a potentially ototoxic medication. What is the audiologist’s most appropriate contribution?
choices: Change the medication dose independently before notifying the prescribing clinician.
Treat the first changed threshold as confirmed ototoxicity and report it without repeating the protocol measure or checking test quality.
Delay communication until the entire treatment course ends regardless of further change.
Confirm the change using the monitoring protocol, document it, and communicate promptly with the medical team and patient.
items.rationale: Ototoxicity monitoring seeks reliable early detection and rapid interdisciplinary communication. The audiologist verifies test quality and change, explains functional implications, and informs the patient and medical team without independently prescribing medication changes.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Medication management lies with the authorized medical prescriber and should not be changed unilaterally by the audiologist. Ototoxicity monitoring seeks reliable early detection and rapid interdisciplinary communication. The audiologist verifies test quality and change, explains functional implications, and informs the patient and medical team without independently prescribing medication changes.

Not the best answer. An apparent change must be confirmed under the monitoring protocol and interpreted with test quality before it is communicated as a shift. Ototoxicity monitoring seeks reliable early detection and rapid interdisciplinary communication. The audiologist verifies test quality and change, explains functional implications, and informs the patient and medical team without independently prescribing medication changes.

Not the best answer. The purpose of monitoring is to support timely discussion and management, not retrospective reporting alone. Ototoxicity monitoring seeks reliable early detection and rapid interdisciplinary communication. The audiologist verifies test quality and change, explains functional implications, and informs the patient and medical team without independently prescribing medication changes.

Correct. Ototoxicity monitoring seeks reliable early detection and rapid interdisciplinary communication. The audiologist verifies test quality and change, explains functional implications, and informs the patient and medical team without independently prescribing medication changes.

Occupational Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation — OSHA

items.prompt: A worker with hearing loss needs protection around machinery but also must detect warning signals. Which plan is most appropriate?
choices: Evaluate measured exposure, communication and alarm demands, device fit and attenuation, and compatible safety accommodations together.
Maximize attenuation without assessing whether critical alarms remain detectable.
Avoid hearing protection because amplification restores normal resistance to hazardous noise.
Rely on coworkers to shout warnings while leaving machinery and procedures unchanged.
items.rationale: Protection for a hearing-impaired worker must control hazardous dose while preserving access to critical communication and warnings. Exposure data, fit, attenuation, alarms, visual or tactile supports, and job procedures should be coordinated rather than optimized in isolation.
choiceRationales: Correct. Protection for a hearing-impaired worker must control hazardous dose while preserving access to critical communication and warnings. Exposure data, fit, attenuation, alarms, visual or tactile supports, and job procedures should be coordinated rather than optimized in isolation.

Not the best answer. Overprotection can interfere with communication and warning access, so attenuation must be matched to actual risk and tasks. Protection for a hearing-impaired worker must control hazardous dose while preserving access to critical communication and warnings. Exposure data, fit, attenuation, alarms, visual or tactile supports, and job procedures should be coordinated rather than optimized in isolation.

Not the best answer. Amplification does not protect the cochlea from hazardous exposure and may require coordinated protection planning. Protection for a hearing-impaired worker must control hazardous dose while preserving access to critical communication and warnings. Exposure data, fit, attenuation, alarms, visual or tactile supports, and job procedures should be coordinated rather than optimized in isolation.

Not the best answer. An informal shouting plan is unreliable and does not constitute a measured, engineered safety approach. Protection for a hearing-impaired worker must control hazardous dose while preserving access to critical communication and warnings. Exposure data, fit, attenuation, alarms, visual or tactile supports, and job procedures should be coordinated rather than optimized in isolation.

Occupational Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation — OSHA

items.prompt: A screening protocol detects nearly every child who truly has the target hearing condition but also refers many children who do not. Which performance pattern is present?
choices: Low sensitivity accompanied by comparatively high specificity.
High sensitivity accompanied by comparatively lower specificity.
High positive predictive value guaranteed in every screened population.
A diagnostic result that determines type and degree of hearing loss.
items.rationale: Sensitivity reflects detection of people who have the condition, whereas specificity reflects correct passes among those who do not. Many false-positive referrals alongside few missed cases indicate high sensitivity with reduced specificity.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Low sensitivity would produce more missed true cases rather than detecting nearly every affected child. Sensitivity reflects detection of people who have the condition, whereas specificity reflects correct passes among those who do not. Many false-positive referrals alongside few missed cases indicate high sensitivity with reduced specificity.

Correct. Sensitivity reflects detection of people who have the condition, whereas specificity reflects correct passes among those who do not. Many false-positive referrals alongside few missed cases indicate high sensitivity with reduced specificity.

Not the best answer. Predictive value also depends on prevalence and is not guaranteed by sensitivity alone. Sensitivity reflects detection of people who have the condition, whereas specificity reflects correct passes among those who do not. Many false-positive referrals alongside few missed cases indicate high sensitivity with reduced specificity.

Not the best answer. Screening performance characteristics do not convert the procedure into a full diagnosis. Sensitivity reflects detection of people who have the condition, whereas specificity reflects correct passes among those who do not. Many false-positive referrals alongside few missed cases indicate high sensitivity with reduced specificity.

items.prompt: A newborn does not pass an initial screen in one ear. Under a protocol calling for an outpatient rescreen, why should both ears be rescreened?
choices: The previously passing ear is assumed to have permanent loss until it fails a second time.
Rescreening both ears transforms the screening procedure into an etiologic diagnosis.
Hearing status or test conditions can change, and a complete bilateral result is needed for current follow-up decisions.
Testing the referred ear would be invalid unless the other ear receives masking noise.
items.rationale: A rescreen is a new bilateral screening event, and transient conditions, delayed changes, or measurement issues may affect either ear. Documenting both ears supports accurate EHDI tracking and appropriate diagnostic referral without treating screening as diagnosis.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A prior pass does not become a presumed permanent loss merely because the opposite ear referred. A rescreen is a new bilateral screening event, and transient conditions, delayed changes, or measurement issues may affect either ear. Documenting both ears supports accurate EHDI tracking and appropriate diagnostic referral without treating screening as diagnosis.

Not the best answer. Bilateral rescreening remains screening and does not establish type, degree, or cause. A rescreen is a new bilateral screening event, and transient conditions, delayed changes, or measurement issues may affect either ear. Documenting both ears supports accurate EHDI tracking and appropriate diagnostic referral without treating screening as diagnosis.

Correct. A rescreen is a new bilateral screening event, and transient conditions, delayed changes, or measurement issues may affect either ear. Documenting both ears supports accurate EHDI tracking and appropriate diagnostic referral without treating screening as diagnosis.

Not the best answer. Routine physiologic newborn rescreening does not require contralateral masking as described. A rescreen is a new bilateral screening event, and transient conditions, delayed changes, or measurement issues may affect either ear. Documenting both ears supports accurate EHDI tracking and appropriate diagnostic referral without treating screening as diagnosis.

items.prompt: A school screening program’s referral rate doubles after testing moves beside a loud ventilation system. What is the best first quality-improvement step?
choices: Conclude that community hearing loss prevalence doubled on the day the room changed.
Lower every child’s recorded threshold until the referral rate returns to its prior value.
Discontinue follow-up referrals while continuing to screen in the same conditions.
Measure ambient noise and review equipment, procedure, and frequency-specific pass criteria before interpreting the increase.
items.rationale: A sudden referral-rate shift that coincides with a noisier site suggests possible low-frequency masking or procedural drift. Ambient-noise verification, calibration checks, protocol review, and repeat testing under valid conditions should precede population conclusions.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The environmental change is a plausible measurement cause that must be evaluated before inferring prevalence. A sudden referral-rate shift that coincides with a noisier site suggests possible low-frequency masking or procedural drift. Ambient-noise verification, calibration checks, protocol review, and repeat testing under valid conditions should precede population conclusions.

Not the best answer. Altering recorded data is invalid and conceals rather than evaluates a quality problem. A sudden referral-rate shift that coincides with a noisier site suggests possible low-frequency masking or procedural drift. Ambient-noise verification, calibration checks, protocol review, and repeat testing under valid conditions should precede population conclusions.

Not the best answer. Suppressing referrals does not correct invalid conditions and may leave children without needed follow-up. A sudden referral-rate shift that coincides with a noisier site suggests possible low-frequency masking or procedural drift. Ambient-noise verification, calibration checks, protocol review, and repeat testing under valid conditions should precede population conclusions.

Correct. A sudden referral-rate shift that coincides with a noisier site suggests possible low-frequency masking or procedural drift. Ambient-noise verification, calibration checks, protocol review, and repeat testing under valid conditions should precede population conclusions.

items.prompt: Tympanometry added to a school screening shows reduced middle-ear mobility for a student who passes pure-tone screening. How should the finding be handled?
choices: Apply the program’s follow-up criteria and avoid diagnosing a hearing-loss type from screening alone.
Diagnose permanent conductive hearing loss solely from the screening tympanogram.
Ignore the result because passing pure tones rules out every middle-ear concern.
Assign a hearing aid immediately without diagnostic evaluation or medical context.
items.rationale: Screening tympanometry can flag possible middle-ear dysfunction, but its meaning depends on otoscopy, history, symptoms, criteria, and follow-up evaluation. The program should communicate the result and next step without overstating diagnostic certainty.
choiceRationales: Correct. Screening tympanometry can flag possible middle-ear dysfunction, but its meaning depends on otoscopy, history, symptoms, criteria, and follow-up evaluation. The program should communicate the result and next step without overstating diagnostic certainty.

Not the best answer. A screening immittance result alone does not establish permanent loss, degree, or etiology. Screening tympanometry can flag possible middle-ear dysfunction, but its meaning depends on otoscopy, history, symptoms, criteria, and follow-up evaluation. The program should communicate the result and next step without overstating diagnostic certainty.

Not the best answer. A pure-tone pass does not invalidate a separate middle-ear screening finding or related symptoms. Screening tympanometry can flag possible middle-ear dysfunction, but its meaning depends on otoscopy, history, symptoms, criteria, and follow-up evaluation. The program should communicate the result and next step without overstating diagnostic certainty.

Not the best answer. Device decisions require comprehensive assessment and cannot rest on one screening measure. Screening tympanometry can flag possible middle-ear dysfunction, but its meaning depends on otoscopy, history, symptoms, criteria, and follow-up evaluation. The program should communicate the result and next step without overstating diagnostic certainty.

items.prompt: An EHDI database shows that many infants received diagnostic evaluations, but the results were not reported back to the program. Which problem is this?
choices: Loss to follow-up because none of the infants received the recommended diagnostic service.
Loss to documentation, which requires closing the reporting gap while protecting data privacy and integrity.
A false-negative screening pattern because every diagnostic result must be normal.
A calibration failure that can be corrected by changing the screening stimulus level.
items.rationale: Loss to documentation occurs when a recommended service is completed but its result is not captured by the tracking system. Standardized secure reporting and reconciliation are needed so the EHDI program can distinguish missing data from missing care.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The scenario says evaluations occurred; the missing element is documentation rather than service completion. Loss to documentation occurs when a recommended service is completed but its result is not captured by the tracking system. Standardized secure reporting and reconciliation are needed so the EHDI program can distinguish missing data from missing care.

Correct. Loss to documentation occurs when a recommended service is completed but its result is not captured by the tracking system. Standardized secure reporting and reconciliation are needed so the EHDI program can distinguish missing data from missing care.

Not the best answer. Missing reports do not establish screening accuracy or the diagnostic outcomes. Loss to documentation occurs when a recommended service is completed but its result is not captured by the tracking system. Standardized secure reporting and reconciliation are needed so the EHDI program can distinguish missing data from missing care.

Not the best answer. The gap is in information exchange and tracking, not necessarily equipment calibration. Loss to documentation occurs when a recommended service is completed but its result is not captured by the tracking system. Standardized secure reporting and reconciliation are needed so the EHDI program can distinguish missing data from missing care.

items.prompt: During a threshold search, a listener responds at 20 dB HL, misses at 15 dB HL, and responds again at 20 dB HL on repeated ascending trials. Which value best represents the behavioral threshold under a 50-percent response criterion?
choices: 15 dB HL because every missed presentation is counted as a positive response.
25 dB HL because threshold must be placed one full step above every reliable response.
20 dB HL, provided the required responses occur on ascending presentations under the clinic protocol.
The arithmetic average of 15, 20, and 20 dB HL regardless of presentation direction.
items.rationale: Behavioral threshold is the lowest level meeting the defined response criterion, commonly based on repeated ascending presentations. Consistent responses at 20 dB HL with nonresponses at 15 dB HL support 20 dB HL when procedural reliability is otherwise acceptable.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A missed presentation is not a detection response and cannot by itself establish threshold. Behavioral threshold is the lowest level meeting the defined response criterion, commonly based on repeated ascending presentations. Consistent responses at 20 dB HL with nonresponses at 15 dB HL support 20 dB HL when procedural reliability is otherwise acceptable.

Not the best answer. Placing threshold above consistent responses overestimates level when the response criterion is already met at 20 dB HL. Behavioral threshold is the lowest level meeting the defined response criterion, commonly based on repeated ascending presentations. Consistent responses at 20 dB HL with nonresponses at 15 dB HL support 20 dB HL when procedural reliability is otherwise acceptable.

Correct. Behavioral threshold is the lowest level meeting the defined response criterion, commonly based on repeated ascending presentations. Consistent responses at 20 dB HL with nonresponses at 15 dB HL support 20 dB HL when procedural reliability is otherwise acceptable.

Not the best answer. Threshold is determined by a response rule, not by averaging response and nonresponse levels. Behavioral threshold is the lowest level meeting the defined response criterion, commonly based on repeated ascending presentations. Consistent responses at 20 dB HL with nonresponses at 15 dB HL support 20 dB HL when procedural reliability is otherwise acceptable.

items.prompt: For an adult with very narrow or collapsing ear canals, which transducer choice can improve threshold validity during air-conduction testing?
choices: A forehead bone oscillator can replace ear-specific air-conduction measures without masking.
A sound-field loudspeaker can establish separate thresholds for the right and left ears.
A supra-aural earphone pressed more firmly against the pinna prevents canal-collapse artifact.
Insert earphones can reduce canal collapse and provide greater interaural attenuation when appropriately fitted and calibrated.
items.rationale: A properly placed insert couples through a foam tip within the canal, often reducing pinna pressure and canal collapse. Inserts also commonly increase interaural attenuation, although calibration, fit, contraindications, and masking rules still apply.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Unmasked bone conduction is not an ear-specific substitute for complete air-conduction assessment. A properly placed insert couples through a foam tip within the canal, often reducing pinna pressure and canal collapse. Inserts also commonly increase interaural attenuation, although calibration, fit, contraindications, and masking rules still apply.

Not the best answer. Sound-field responses reflect the better-hearing pathway and are not independently ear specific. A properly placed insert couples through a foam tip within the canal, often reducing pinna pressure and canal collapse. Inserts also commonly increase interaural attenuation, although calibration, fit, contraindications, and masking rules still apply.

Not the best answer. Additional pressure may worsen collapse and does not reliably solve the anatomical problem. A properly placed insert couples through a foam tip within the canal, often reducing pinna pressure and canal collapse. Inserts also commonly increase interaural attenuation, although calibration, fit, contraindications, and masking rules still apply.

Correct. A properly placed insert couples through a foam tip within the canal, often reducing pinna pressure and canal collapse. Inserts also commonly increase interaural attenuation, although calibration, fit, contraindications, and masking rules still apply.

items.prompt: A toddler responds to warble tones from a loudspeaker at 25 dB HL. What is the most defensible way to report the response?
choices: As a sound-field minimum response level that is not independently assignable to either ear.
As a confirmed 25 dB HL threshold for each ear separately.
As an exact bone-conduction threshold for the poorer cochlea.
As proof that tympanometry and otoacoustic emissions will be normal bilaterally.
items.rationale: Sound-field testing stimulates both ears and generally reflects whichever pathway detects the signal more effectively. With a young child, responses may also be minimum response levels rather than adult-like thresholds, so ear-specific cross-checks remain necessary.
choiceRationales: Correct. Sound-field testing stimulates both ears and generally reflects whichever pathway detects the signal more effectively. With a young child, responses may also be minimum response levels rather than adult-like thresholds, so ear-specific cross-checks remain necessary.

Not the best answer. A single loudspeaker response cannot establish the sensitivity of each ear independently. Sound-field testing stimulates both ears and generally reflects whichever pathway detects the signal more effectively. With a young child, responses may also be minimum response levels rather than adult-like thresholds, so ear-specific cross-checks remain necessary.

Not the best answer. Airborne sound-field stimulation does not yield an ear-specific bone-conduction threshold. Sound-field testing stimulates both ears and generally reflects whichever pathway detects the signal more effectively. With a young child, responses may also be minimum response levels rather than adult-like thresholds, so ear-specific cross-checks remain necessary.

Not the best answer. A behavioral sound-field response does not determine middle-ear or OAE findings. Sound-field testing stimulates both ears and generally reflects whichever pathway detects the signal more effectively. With a young child, responses may also be minimum response levels rather than adult-like thresholds, so ear-specific cross-checks remain necessary.

items.prompt: An adult shows a large unexplained unilateral pure-tone asymmetry, and reliability concerns remain after reinstruction. Which behavioral procedure may contribute to the cross-check?
choices: A bilateral caloric test comparing low-frequency horizontal-canal responses.
A properly administered Stenger test using simultaneous same-frequency tones at controlled interaural levels.
A tympanometric width measure used as the sole indicator of behavioral honesty.
An aided speech-in-noise test performed without verifying the asymmetric thresholds.
items.rationale: The Stenger principle can help evaluate certain unilateral or markedly asymmetric nonorganic response patterns by presenting identical-frequency stimuli to both ears at different levels. It is one cross-check and must be interpreted with the complete behavioral and physiologic record.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Caloric testing evaluates vestibular function and does not cross-check unilateral pure-tone response reliability. The Stenger principle can help evaluate certain unilateral or markedly asymmetric nonorganic response patterns by presenting identical-frequency stimuli to both ears at different levels. It is one cross-check and must be interpreted with the complete behavioral and physiologic record.

Correct. The Stenger principle can help evaluate certain unilateral or markedly asymmetric nonorganic response patterns by presenting identical-frequency stimuli to both ears at different levels. It is one cross-check and must be interpreted with the complete behavioral and physiologic record.

Not the best answer. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear mechanics and cannot determine behavioral response intent by itself. The Stenger principle can help evaluate certain unilateral or markedly asymmetric nonorganic response patterns by presenting identical-frequency stimuli to both ears at different levels. It is one cross-check and must be interpreted with the complete behavioral and physiologic record.

Not the best answer. Aided speech testing does not directly resolve an unexplained unilateral behavioral threshold asymmetry. The Stenger principle can help evaluate certain unilateral or markedly asymmetric nonorganic response patterns by presenting identical-frequency stimuli to both ears at different levels. It is one cross-check and must be interpreted with the complete behavioral and physiologic record.

Guidelines for Audiology Service Provision in and for Schools — ASHA

items.prompt: A word-recognition performance-intensity function improves to a maximum and then declines substantially as presentation level rises. What should the audiologist do?
choices: Report the lowest score as the client’s speech-recognition threshold.
Average all percentages and diagnose a conductive disorder from that number.
Verify the finding and interpret possible rollover with the complete audiologic and medical-referral context.
Increase level without output limits until the score returns to one hundred percent.
items.rationale: A marked decline from the maximum word-recognition score at higher levels can be described as rollover, but it requires confirmation of presentation, scoring, audibility, distortion, and reliability. The pattern contributes to, rather than replaces, broader diagnostic and referral reasoning.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A suprathreshold word score is not an SRT, and choosing the lowest score ignores the performance-intensity function. A marked decline from the maximum word-recognition score at higher levels can be described as rollover, but it requires confirmation of presentation, scoring, audibility, distortion, and reliability. The pattern contributes to, rather than replaces, broader diagnostic and referral reasoning.

Not the best answer. Averaging the scores does not isolate rollover and cannot by itself diagnose middle-ear pathology. A marked decline from the maximum word-recognition score at higher levels can be described as rollover, but it requires confirmation of presentation, scoring, audibility, distortion, and reliability. The pattern contributes to, rather than replaces, broader diagnostic and referral reasoning.

Correct. A marked decline from the maximum word-recognition score at higher levels can be described as rollover, but it requires confirmation of presentation, scoring, audibility, distortion, and reliability. The pattern contributes to, rather than replaces, broader diagnostic and referral reasoning.

Not the best answer. Escalating level without safety limits is inappropriate and may not reverse a clinically meaningful decline. A marked decline from the maximum word-recognition score at higher levels can be described as rollover, but it requires confirmation of presentation, scoring, audibility, distortion, and reliability. The pattern contributes to, rather than replaces, broader diagnostic and referral reasoning.

Guidelines for Audiology Service Provision in and for Schools — ASHA

items.prompt: A bilingual child is unfamiliar with the vocabulary in an open-set English speech test. Which modification best protects interpretation?
choices: Interpret every missed English word as evidence of reduced cochlear sensitivity.
Translate individual test words spontaneously and apply the original monolingual norms unchanged.
Skip case history and rely on the English percentage as a language-neutral measure.
Select linguistically appropriate materials or response formats and document how language familiarity limits comparison with norms.
items.rationale: Speech testing should match the purpose, language experience, and available valid materials. Closed-set or appropriately adapted tasks may reduce lexical demands, but all departures from standardized conditions and resulting interpretive limits must be documented.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. Vocabulary unfamiliarity can lower scores without reflecting peripheral sensitivity. Speech testing should match the purpose, language experience, and available valid materials. Closed-set or appropriately adapted tasks may reduce lexical demands, but all departures from standardized conditions and resulting interpretive limits must be documented.

Not the best answer. Ad hoc translation changes stimulus properties, so the original standardization cannot simply be retained. Speech testing should match the purpose, language experience, and available valid materials. Closed-set or appropriately adapted tasks may reduce lexical demands, but all departures from standardized conditions and resulting interpretive limits must be documented.

Not the best answer. Speech recognition is influenced by linguistic experience and must be interpreted with language history. Speech testing should match the purpose, language experience, and available valid materials. Closed-set or appropriately adapted tasks may reduce lexical demands, but all departures from standardized conditions and resulting interpretive limits must be documented.

Correct. Speech testing should match the purpose, language experience, and available valid materials. Closed-set or appropriately adapted tasks may reduce lexical demands, but all departures from standardized conditions and resulting interpretive limits must be documented.

Guidelines for Audiology Service Provision in and for Schools — ASHA

items.prompt: Pure-tone thresholds obtained today are 15 dB poorer than last week at several frequencies, but the client reports no change. What is the best first response?
choices: Recheck instructions, transducer placement, equipment status, test environment, and threshold reliability before concluding a true shift.
Diagnose permanent progression from the numerical difference without repeating a measure.
Replace both audiograms with their arithmetic average and omit the discrepancy.
Assume the earlier thresholds are correct because the first test in a series has priority.
items.rationale: Threshold change can reflect hearing status, placement, calibration, noise, instructions, attention, or response variability. Immediate procedural and cross-check review distinguishes measurement error from a repeatable change that warrants clinical follow-up.
choiceRationales: Correct. Threshold change can reflect hearing status, placement, calibration, noise, instructions, attention, or response variability. Immediate procedural and cross-check review distinguishes measurement error from a repeatable change that warrants clinical follow-up.

Not the best answer. Unexpected change requires validation before a permanent clinical conclusion is made. Threshold change can reflect hearing status, placement, calibration, noise, instructions, attention, or response variability. Immediate procedural and cross-check review distinguishes measurement error from a repeatable change that warrants clinical follow-up.

Not the best answer. Averaging conceals a potentially important validity or clinical issue rather than resolving it. Threshold change can reflect hearing status, placement, calibration, noise, instructions, attention, or response variability. Immediate procedural and cross-check review distinguishes measurement error from a repeatable change that warrants clinical follow-up.

Not the best answer. Chronology alone does not establish which result is valid. Threshold change can reflect hearing status, placement, calibration, noise, instructions, attention, or response variability. Immediate procedural and cross-check review distinguishes measurement error from a repeatable change that warrants clinical follow-up.
